none: Mt. Saint Helens recovery operation (WA,OR) : environmental impact statement.

After the 1980 eruption of Mt. Saint Helens, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers proposed a recovery operation to address the flooding and debris issues in Cowlitz County, Washington, and Columbia County, Oregon. The plan involved dredging to restore navigation channels in the Columbia and Cowlitz Rivers, removing sediment and debris, and constructing debris retaining structures on the Toutle River. The goal was to prevent further flooding and restore river navigation before the wet season. This effort aimed to protect human life and property while minimizing environmental impacts.
Mt. Saint Helens recovery operation (WA,OR) : environmental impact statement.

After the 1980 eruption of Mt. Saint Helens, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers proposed a recovery operation to address the flooding and debris issues in Cowlitz County, Washington, and Columbia County, Oregon. The plan involved dredging to restore navigation channels in the Columbia and Cowlitz Rivers, removing sediment and debris, and constructing debris retaining structures on the Toutle River. The goal was to prevent further flooding and restore river navigation before the wet season. This effort aimed to protect human life and property while minimizing environmental impacts.
Cowlitz County, Washington and Columbia County, Oregon
U.S. Army Corps of Engineers, Portland District
U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, Region X
U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, Region I

The recovery operation was proposed in response to the devastating eruption of Mt. Saint Helens in 1980, which caused significant flooding and debris flow issues in the surrounding areas. There was some public concern regarding the environmental impacts of the proposed dredging and construction activities. Ultimately, the project aimed to restore navigation and prevent future flooding, balancing the need for infrastructure with environmental considerations.
United States. Army. Corps of Engineers
"Many residents worry or have anxiety about extent of winter floods. Low-lying areas will engage in emergency preparations." — Section 4.5.1.1 – No Action

"The therapeutic community recedes when disaster victims have received emergency aid, most people return to their homes, and the most obvious repair and cleanup tasks have been accomplished." — Section 4.5.1 – Social Effects Associated with Natural Disasters
